did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the Inception v2 deep learning architecture to classify dermoscopic images of skin cancer (benign vs. malignant) from the ISIC archive. The study applied image preprocessing techniques and evaluated the classification performance, achieving an accuracy of 88.66%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the application of the Inception v2 deep learning architecture to dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive. It details specific methodology including image preprocessing (color clarification, edge detection, noise removal), dataset composition (2437 training images, 660 test images), and reports new empirical results, specifically an accuracy improvement of 3.33 points to 88.66% and an F1 score of 88.25%.

title: Improvement of Skin Cancer Detection Performance Using Deep Learning Technique
brief_summary: This study aimed to improve the classification performance of benign and malignant skin cancer using deep learning techniques on dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive. The researchers applied image preprocessing steps, including color clarification, edge detection, and noise removal, to enhance lesion features before classification. The Inception v2 deep learning architecture was employed to classify the processed images into two categories. The evaluation compared the model's accuracy with and without preprocessing, training the network for 40 epochs with a decaying learning rate. The key result showed that preprocessing increased the accuracy by 3.33 percentage points, achieving a final accuracy of 88.66% and an F1 score of 88.25%.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classification classes used in the experiment. The dataset contained 1330 benign training images and 360 benign test images. Reported accuracy for this class was 93.05% (without preprocessing) and 87.87% (with preprocessing).

One of the two binary classification classes used in the experiment. The dataset contained 1107 malignant training images and 300 malignant test images. Reported accuracy for this class was 85.33% (without preprocessing) and 88.66% (with preprocessing). The paper specifically notes that malignant skin cancer is the most dangerous type and mentions melanoma as a type of malignant cancer in the introduction.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into benign and malignant skin cancer categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper uses the Inception v2 deep learning architecture to classify dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive into two discrete classes: benign (iyi huylu) and malignant (kötü huylu) skin cancer. The study reports accuracy rates for each class (e.g., 88.66% for malignant with preprocessing) and an overall F1 score of 88.25%, confirming a binary classification task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images acquired from the ISIC archive (https://www.isic-archive.com/). The images are 224x224x3 pixels in size.

use.training: Used to train the Inception v2 deep learning architecture for binary classification of skin cancer.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the classification performance of the model with and without image preprocessing.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance improvements were described using absolute percentage point differences (e.g., a 3.33 point increase in accuracy) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Archive.experiment: Binary classification of benign vs malignant skin cancer using Inception v2
ISIC Archive.notes: The application of image preprocessing increased the accuracy for malignant skin cancer detection by 3.33 percentage points (from 85.33% to 88.66%). Training loss stabilized after 25 epochs for both experiments. Accuracy saturation was reached after 25 epochs for the no-preprocessing model and after 35 epochs for the preprocessing model.

architecture_details.description: Deep convolutional neural network developed by GoogleNet. The architecture uses modules containing parallel convolutional layers of different sizes (1x1, 3x3, 5x5) and max-pooling layers. Specifically, Inception v2 replaces the 5x5 convolutional layer found in Inception v1 with two sequential 3x3 convolutional layers to reduce computational load.

preprocessing_techniques: Image contrast improvement via thresholding (görüntü kontrastını iyileştirme için eşikleme)
Color space transformation (HSV - Hue, Saturation and Value) (renk uzayı dönüşümü)
conclusions: Applying image preprocessing techniques (color clarification, edge detection, and noise removal) to dermoscopic images improved the classification performance of the Inception v2 deep learning model for skin cancer detection.
The application of preprocessing increased the accuracy for malignant skin cancer detection by 3.33 percentage points, raising it from 85.33% to 88.66%.
The final model achieved an overall accuracy of 88.66% and an F1 score of 88.25% when preprocessing was applied.
Without preprocessing, the Inception v2 model achieved an accuracy of 93.05% for benign lesions and 85.33% for malignant lesions, with an F1 score of 89.55%.
With preprocessing, the accuracy for benign lesions decreased slightly to 87.87%, while the accuracy for malignant lesions increased to 88.66%.

Training loss stabilized after 25 epochs for both the model with and without preprocessing, while accuracy saturation was reached after 25 epochs for the no-preprocessing model and after 35 epochs for the preprocessing model.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the ISIC archive dataset which may introduce population or dataset bias.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the reported 3.33 percentage point improvement in accuracy.

The preprocessing pipeline (color clarification, edge detection, noise removal) improved malignant detection accuracy but decreased benign detection accuracy (from 93.05% to 87.87%), indicating a trade-off in class-specific performance.
The dataset is small (3,097 total images) and binary (benign vs. malignant), limiting the generalizability to multi-class skin cancer classification or other dermatological conditions.
The paper does not report data leakage risks or detailed cross-validation strategies, relying on a single train/test split.
Clinical relevance is limited as the model is a binary classifier and does not replace the need for pathological analysis or expert dermatological consultation.
